figures.caption: Table 2 | Main evaluation results on V IEWSUITE. Proprietary: GPT (OpenAI, 2023), Gemini (Gemini Team et al., 2023), Claude (Anthropic, 2024), Grok. Open-weight: Qwen (Bai et al., 2025), GLM (Zeng et al., 2024), Kimi. Accuracy (%) shown for Short / Long / Overall splits. Models sorted by Overall within each group; best per column in bold.
Figure 4 | Point cloud coverage across planning turns. (a) Scene coverage. (b) Target intersection.
Figure 9 | Spearman 𝜌 between sample-level factors (rows) and per-model success (columns). Factors grouped into geometric distance, visual overlap, and directional geometry.
Figure 5 | Distribution of unified view distance 𝑑 across 530 test pairs. The threshold 𝑑 = 3 separates SHORT (185 pairs) and LONG (345 pairs) subsets.
Figure 11 | Point cloud coverage across all 15 models. (a) Scene coverage ratio. (b) Target intersection ratio.
Table 1 | Comparison with existing view reasoning benchmarks. ∗EmbodiedBench supports multiple action types.

Table 3 | Effects of turn budget and rendering quality. Left three blocks: IVP accuracy (%) under increasing turn budgets with point-cloud rendering. Rightmost block: P2V / V2P / IVP All-split accuracy at budget=10 with higher-fidelity Gaussian Splat (GS) rendering.
Figure 2 | Success rate vs. rotation distance (top) and position distance (bottom) for proprietary models across all three tasks.
Figure 6 | P2V example. Action: [turn_right × 5]. GPT-5.4 Pro selects C (incorrect).
Table 4 | IVP success rates (%) across prompting and training baselines, ablations, and our methods.

Figure 1 | Overview of V IEWSUITE. Left: Point cloud environment built on ScanNet with viewpoints (initial in blue, target in orange) and rendered first-person views. Middle: Path-toView (P2V; predict the resulting view from an action sequence) and View-to-Path (V2P; infer the action sequence from two views), both single-turn. Right: Interactive View Planning (IVP), a multi-turn task where the agent plans view changes to match the target view and submits a viewpoint estimate.
Table 7 | Data pipeline hyperparameters.

Table 9 | IVP success rates (%) under the default protocol and two evaluation-protocol ablations. Default: per-step rotations are snapped to integer multiples of 𝑠𝑟=30◦, and success requires an explicit submit within the unified-distance threshold; numbers are reproduced from Table 2 and Table 4. No-Snap: rotations are not snapped; raw rotation magnitudes are executed as-is. No-Submit: no explicit submit required; an episode is counted as successful as soon as its pose falls within the unified-distance threshold of the target. “Ours” denotes our fully-trained models (Section 5.2).
Figure 7 | V2P example. GPT-5.4 Pro selects B (correct): [turn_left × 5, move_left].
Table 11 | SFT training hyperparameters for our framework.
Figure 10 | Action frequency distribution across iterations. The base policy (iter 0) favors move_forward; later iterations shift toward rotations and more balanced translations.

Figure 3 | Iterative training pipeline. Left: in the self-exploration stage, the agent actively explores V IEWSUITE environments under sparse outcome rewards; completed trajectories are continuously compressed into a view graph whose nodes are viewpoints and whose edges are actions. Right: in the view graph distillation stage, paths sampled from the graph are reformulated into multi-turn view-planning demonstrations and auxiliary supervision (viewdifference estimation and multiple-choice). The distilled model initializes the self-exploration iteration, progressively bootstrapping the policy.
Table 6 | Detailed action definitions. All rotations are about the camera center in local coordinates.
Figure 13 | Image attention fraction across all 28 layers. Both models evaluated on the same 530 trajectories.
Table 10 | RL training hyperparameters for our framework and baselines.
Figure 8 | IVP example. Our trained Qwen2.5-VL-7B plans view changes from (b) to match (a) in 6 turns. Final viewpoint error: 0.061 m / 0◦. Success.
Figure 12 | (a) Total rollouts by turn count. (b) Successful rollouts. (c) Success rate.
Table 8 | Success threshold calibration on IVP rollouts. Each row evaluates the threshold-based success indicator at one (position, rotation) threshold pair against the human label.
